questions.text: Total transpiration which can take place through stomata is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While this option indicates a significant portion of transpiration, it still underestimates the actual contribution of stomata. This range is slightly lower than the true percentage attributed to stomatal transpiration, which is higher.
This value greatly underestimates the role of stomata, which are responsible for the majority of transpiration in plants. The actual percentage is substantially higher than this range.

This option is correct. Stomata are responsible for the majority of water vapor loss in plants, accounting for approximately 80-90% of total transpiration, highlighting their vital role in water regulation.
This option underestimates the contribution of stomata to total transpiration. The percentage of water loss through stomata is significantly higher than this range.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 80-90%. Stomata serve as the main openings for water vapor release during transpiration, making them the primary pathway for water loss in plants. This high percentage reflects the critical role of stomata in regulating water loss. Other options, such as 1-2% and 5-7%, vastly underestimate the significant contribution of stomatal transpiration. The 60-70% option is closer, but still does not accurately capture the full impact of stomatal transpiration.

questions.text: Vessels are found in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Factually incorrect.

options.text: Most of angiosperms and few gymnosperms
options.explanation: This option is correct because Vessels are mostly found in angiospermic plants. Since angiosperms are known as flowering plants most angiosperms without any exception unlike gymnosperms have a water-conducting vessel.

options.text: All angiosperms, all gymnosperms and some pteridophyta
options.explanation: This option is correct because Vessels are mostly found in angiospermic plants. Since angiosperms are known as flowering plants most angiosperms without any exception unlike gymnosperms have a water-conducting vessel.

This option is correct because Vessels are mostly found in angiospermic plants. Since angiosperms are known as flowering plants most angiosperms without any exception unlike gymnosperms have a water-conducting vessel.

questions.explanation: Vessels are long tubelike structures ideally suited for the conduction of water and solutes. These are made up of a row of cylindrical cells arranged in longitudinal series. The partition walls of these cells are perforated and as such the entire structure becomes tubelike. The region of the wall where perforations occur is known as the perforation plate. Vessels are found in the wood of almost all the angiosperms except certain primitive members of the order ranales (vessels dicots), e.g., Trochodendron, Tetracentron, Drimys, Pseudowintera, etc. Vessels also occur in some pteridophytes, such as Selaginella, and in the members of order Gnetales of gymnosperms (e.g., Genetum, Ephedra, and Welwitschia).

questions.text: Which is correct about the transport of conduction of substances?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Organic food moves up through phloem
options.explanation: Protoplasmic streaming occurs in sieve tube elements of phloem and the solute molecules caught up in the circulating cytoplasm are carried from one end to the other end of sieve tube from where they diffuse to the next sieve tube elements through the cytoplasmic strands in the sieve plates.

options.text: Organic food moves up through xylem
options.explanation: Protoplasmic streaming occurs in sieve tube elements of phloem and the solute molecules caught up in the circulating cytoplasm are carried from one end to the other end of sieve tube from where they diffuse to the next sieve tube elements through the cytoplasmic strands in the sieve plates.

options.text: Inorganic food moves upwardly and downwardly through xylem
options.explanation: Protoplasmic streaming occurs in sieve tube elements of phloem and the solute molecules caught up in the circulating cytoplasm are carried from one end to the other end of sieve tube from where they diffuse to the next sieve tube elements through the cytoplasmic strands in the sieve plates.

options.text: Organic food moves upwardly and downwardly through phloem
options.explanation: This option is correct because The movement of organic food materials or the solutes in soluble from one place to another in higher plants is called as translocation of organic solutes. Protoplasmic streaming occurs in sieve tube elements of phloem and the solute molecules caught up in the circulating cytoplasm are carried from one end to the other end of sieve tube from where they diffuse to the next sieve tube elements through the cytoplasmic strands in the sieve plates.

questions.explanation: Phloem is the food-conducting tissue of plants. The sieve tubes are food-conducting elements of the plants. It is proposed that food is translocated by mass flow or by streaming currents of protoplasm. The movement of organic food materials or the solutes in soluble from one place to another in higher plants is called as translocation of organic solutes. Protoplasmic streaming occurs in sieve tube elements of phloem and the solute molecules caught up in the circulating cytoplasm are carried from one end to the other end of the sieve tube from where they diffuse to the next sieve tube elements through the cytoplasmic strands in the sieve plates.

questions.text: Apoplast pathway can take water and minerals up to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The apoplast pathway is a route for water and minerals to move through the non-living spaces of plant tissues, such as the cell walls. While the apoplast pathway can transport water and minerals through the cortex and endodermis, it is stopped by the Casparian strip in the endodermis, preventing direct entry into the xylem. Therefore, this option is not entirely correct.

The apoplast pathway refers to movement through cell walls and non-living spaces. While the cortex is part of the plant tissue, the apoplast pathway extends beyond the cortex to the endodermis. The Casparian strip in the endodermis prevents further apoplastic movement beyond this point. Therefore, this option is not entirely correct.

Correct. The apoplast pathway can transport water and minerals up to the endodermis, which is a specialized layer of cells in the root. However, the Casparian strip in the endodermis acts as a barrier, stopping the apoplast pathway from continuing into the xylem. Water and minerals must enter the symplast pathway by crossing the endodermal cell membranes to reach the xylem.

The apoplast pathway can extend up to the pericycle, a tissue layer just inside the endodermis. However, it is important to note that the apoplast pathway is stopped by the Casparian strip in the endodermis, preventing direct transport of water and minerals from the pericycle to the xylem. Therefore, this option is not entirely correct.

questions.explanation: The apoplast pathway can transport water and minerals up to the endodermis, which is a specialized layer of cells in the root. However, the Casparian strip in the endodermis acts as a barrier, stopping the apoplast pathway from continuing into the xylem. Water and minerals must enter the symplast pathway by crossing the endodermal cell membranes to reach the xylem.

questions.text: The pathway adopted by water in plants through adjacent cell walls is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. The apoplast pathway is the route taken by water in plants through the non-living spaces of adjacent cell walls. In the apoplast pathway, water moves freely through the cell walls without crossing cell membranes. It is an important pathway for the movement of water and minerals within plants.

The symplast pathway involves the movement of water and solutes through the living parts of cells, such as the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata (channels connecting adjacent cells). While the symplast pathway is crucial for the overall movement of substances within plants, it is not specifically related to water movement through adjacent cell walls.
The vacuolar pathway is not a commonly recognized term in plant physiology. The vacuole is a storage organelle within plant cells, and its role is different from that of the apoplast pathway.

The tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole and is involved in regulating the movement of substances in and out of the vacuole. However, it does not specifically refer to the pathway of water through adjacent cell walls.

questions.explanation: The apoplast pathway is the route taken by water in plants through the non-living spaces of adjacent cell walls. In the apoplast pathway, water moves freely through the cell walls without crossing cell membranes. It is an important pathway for the movement of water and minerals within plants.

questions.text: In the root cells _ pathway becomes discontinuous in the endodermis due to the presence of casparian strips.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole and is not directly related to the discontinuity of the apoplast pathway in the endodermis.

This option is correct. In the root cells, the apoplast pathway becomes discontinuous in the endodermis due to the presence of Casparian strips. The Casparian strips are waxy barriers made of suberin that are present in the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells. They block the apoplastic movement of water, nutrients, and ions, forcing substances to enter the symplast pathway by crossing the cell membranes of the endodermal cells.
The symplast pathway involves movement through the living parts of cells and is not directly affected by the presence of Casparian strips.

The vacuolar pathway is the movement of water molecules in plant cells via the vacuoles located in the cytoplasm of the cell but it is not related to the discontinuity of the apoplast pathway in the endodermis.

questions.explanation: In the root cells, the apoplast pathway becomes discontinuous in the endodermis due to the presence of Casparian strips. The Casparian strips are waxy barriers made of suberin that are present in the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells. They block the apoplastic movement of water, nutrients, and ions, forcing substances to enter the symplast pathway by crossing the cell membranes of the endodermal cells.

questions.text: Sap ascends in woody plants because of the root pressure and
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In woody plants, the primary mechanism responsible for the ascent of sap is the transpirational pull. Transpiration, the evaporation of water from the leaves, creates a negative pressure or tension in the xylem vessels. This tension pulls water molecules upward from the roots to replace the lost water, resulting in a continuous flow of sap.

Capillarity is the phenomenon of liquid rising in narrow tubes due to adhesive and cohesive forces. While capillarity plays a role in the movement of water within the xylem vessels, it is not the primary driving force for the long-distance transport of sap in woody plants.

Molecular adhesion refers to the attraction between water molecules and the cell walls of the xylem vessels. Adhesion helps water to cling to the vessel walls, preventing it from collapsing under tension. While important for maintaining the integrity of the xylem, it does not directly contribute to the ascent of sap.

Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert light energy into chemical energy to produce glucose. While photosynthesis is crucial for the plant's energy needs, it is not directly involved in the ascent of sap in woody plants.

questions.explanation: In woody plants, the primary mechanism responsible for the ascent of sap is the transpirational pull. Transpiration, the evaporation of water from the leaves, creates a negative pressure or tension in the xylem vessels. This tension pulls water molecules upward from the roots to replace the lost water, resulting in a continuous flow of sap.

questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Concentration of solutes in the cell sap is high
options.explanation: The concentration of water in the soil is greater than the concentration of water in the root hairs. This means that the water potential in the soil must be lower than the water potential in the root hairs.
This phenomenon is irrelevant to the movement of water molecules in the root, water potential is the driving force not the rapidly respiring plant.

options.text: They are separated from soil by a permeable membrane
options.explanation: The root hairs are separated from the soil by a permeable membrane. This membrane allows water and nutrients to pass through, but it prevents larger molecules, such as salts, from passing through.The permeable membrane is made up of a layer of cells that are joined together by tiny pores. These pores are small enough to allow water and nutrients to pass through, but they are too small for larger molecules, such as salts, to pass through.The permeable membrane is an important part of the root hair's ability to absorb water and nutrients from the soil. Without the permeable membrane, the root hairs would not be able to take up the water and nutrients that the plant needs to survive.

options.text: Concentration of salts in the soil is high
options.explanation: When the concentration of salts in the soil is high, it can be difficult for plants to absorb water. This is because the salts attract water molecules, which means that there is less water available for the plants to take up.

questions.text: Osmosis is the movement of
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Solute particles from higher concentration to lower concentration
options.explanation: Solute particles will always move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. This is because the solute particles are randomly moving and they are more likely to collide with other solute particles in an area of high concentration than in an area of low concentration.This process is called diffusion.

options.text: Solvent particles from higher water potential to lower water potential
options.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from an area of high water potential to an area of low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane.

options.text: Solute particles from higher concentration to lower concentration through a semipermeable membrane
options.explanation: Diffusion is the movement of solute particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. This movement occurs because the solute particles are randomly moving and they are more likely to collide with other solute particles in an area of high concentration than in an area of low concentration.

options.text: Solvent particles from lower water potential to higher water potential
options.explanation: There is no such thing as solvent particles moving from lower water potential to higher water potential. Solvent particles, such as water, always move from an area of high water potential to an area of low water potential. This is because water is attracted to areas where there is less water.

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules from an area of high water potential to an area of low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane.

questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When cells become flaccid they close. Hence, the transpiration rate decreases.
When cells become turgid they remain open and transpiration increases. Hence, this option increases.
When collapsed guard cells will not be able to regulate transpiration at all. Hence, this option is incorrect.
When ruptured guard cells will not be able to regulate transpitation at all. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: When cells become flaccid they close. Hence, the transpiration rate decreases.

questions.text: A plant requires nitrogen and sulphur for its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The formation of cell walls primarily involves cellulose, which is made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Nitrogen and sulfur are not directly involved in cell wall composition.
Incorrect. Starch is a carbohydrate and is made up of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Nitrogen and sulfur do not play a direct role in starch storage.

Correct. Nitrogen and sulfur are vital components of many enzymes in plants. Enzymes are proteins, which contain nitrogen and sulfur in their amino acid structure, facilitating numerous biochemical reactions.
Partially related but incorrect for sulfur. Nitrogen is a component of nitrogenous bases in DNA, but sulfur is not involved in DNA replication. Sulfur is primarily found in proteins and enzymes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Enzyme'. Nitrogen and sulfur are crucial for the formation of enzymes, which are proteins. These elements are part of the amino acids that make up proteins, allowing enzymes to catalyze essential biochemical reactions in plants. The other options are incorrect because:Cell wall: Composed mainly of cellulose, not directly involving nitrogen or sulfur.Starch deposit: A carbohydrate, not requiring nitrogen or sulfur for its composition.DNA replication: Relies on nitrogen for base formation, but sulfur is not directly involved.

questions.text: Which of the following process causes substances to move across membranes without the expenditure of cellular energy?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Endocytosis is an active transport process where cells engulf substances into vesicles, requiring energy (ATP) to facilitate the process.
This option is correct. Diffusion is a passive transport mechanism where substances move from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration without the need for cellular energy.
This option is incorrect. Active transport involves the movement of substances against their concentration gradient, which requires cellular energy (ATP) to occur.
This option is incorrect. Diffusion is indeed a process that moves substances across membranes without cellular energy, making it the correct answer.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Diffusion. Diffusion is characterized by the passive movement of molecules from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration, which occurs naturally and does not require energy input from the cell. In contrast, Endocytosis and Active Transport both require energy because they involve active movement of substances against their concentration gradients. Therefore, the only process listed that matches the criteria of not using cellular energy is diffusion.

questions.text: Most of mycorrhizae are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Mycorrhizal associations are more widespread than just in 50% of vascular plants. They are found in a higher percentage, especially among angiosperms.
This is an underestimate. Approximately 90% of angiosperms form mycorrhizal associations, making this option incorrect.
This is incorrect. Mycorrhizal associations are less common in gymnosperms compared to angiosperms, and 70% is not an accurate representation of their prevalence in gymnosperms.
This is the correct option. Mycorrhizae are symbiotic associations between fungi and plant roots, and approximately 90% of angiosperms form these relationships to enhance nutrient uptake.

questions.explanation: Answer is “90% of angiosperms”Explanation: Mycorrhizal fungi get sugar and shelter from the plant, and in exchange increase the plant’s mineral nutrient uptake efficiency. Mycorrhizae are present in 90% families of flowering plants.

questions.text: Hydathodes are associated with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Transpiration involves the loss of water vapor from the aerial parts of plants, mainly through stomata, not hydathodes. It is driven by evaporation and not related to hydathodes.
Conduction refers to the process of heat transfer, typically in the context of physics, not related to the function of hydathodes in plants.
Guttation is the process by which plants exude liquid water through hydathodes, usually seen as droplets on the edges of leaves. It is caused by root pressure and is distinct from transpiration.
Deplasmolysis is a process occurring in plant cells where the cell regains its turgidity after having lost water. It involves osmotic movement of water, but is not related to hydathodes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Guttation. Hydathodes are specialized structures on leaves that facilitate guttation, which is the exudation of liquid water from the plant, typically at night or in the early morning when transpiration is low, and root pressure is high. In contrast, transpiration is the loss of water vapor from the plant and involves stomata, not hydathodes. Conduction is unrelated to hydathodes as it pertains to heat transfer. Deplasmolysis is the process of regaining turgor in plant cells, which involves water movement but does not involve hydathodes.

questions.text: The xylem water tension is strong enough to pull water to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the correct option. Xylem can generate sufficient tension through the process of transpiration to transport water from the roots to the leaves, reaching heights up to 200 meters in some plant species, particularly tall trees. This is due to the cohesion and adhesion properties of water.
400 feet is approximately 122 meters, which is a possible height for water transport in many trees, but it does not reach the maximum potential height of xylem tension, which is around 200 meters.
300 meters exceeds the generally accepted maximum range for xylem tension in plants. Typically, the maximum height is around 200 meters due to the limitations of water cohesion and adhesion forces.
500 feet is approximately 152 meters, which is within the range of xylem tension but still less than the maximum height of 200 meters observed in some plant species.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is "200 meters." Xylem water tension, a result of transpiration and the cohesive and adhesive properties of water, can pull water to great heights in plants, up to around 200 meters. This limit is determined by the physical properties of water and the structure of the xylem. The other options represent heights that are either within a typical range but not the maximum (400 feet, 500 feet) or exceed the maximum observed in nature (300 meters).

questions.text: 1% of the absorbed water is used by plants in its activities during:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Metabolism involves a wide range of chemical reactions in plants, some of which use water. However, the percentage of water used for overall metabolism is not typically quantified as 1% of absorbed water.

While water is involved in respiration, the specific 1% figure does not apply to this process. Respiration primarily involves the exchange of gases and does not consume a significant portion of the absorbed water.

This is the correct option. During photosynthesis, plants utilize approximately 1% of the absorbed water to convert carbon dioxide and light into glucose and oxygen. The majority of absorbed water is used in transpiration or as a solvent.
Vernalization is a cold treatment process that induces flowering in some plants. It does not relate to water usage and is not linked to the 1% water figure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Photosynthesis. During photosynthesis, plants use about 1% of the absorbed water to convert carbon dioxide and light into glucose and oxygen. This process is critical for plant growth and energy production. Other options, like metabolism and respiration, involve water, but they do not specifically use 1% of the absorbed water. Vernalization is unrelated to water usage as it pertains to the flowering process induced by cold exposure.

questions.text: In tall trees, large quantities of water are carried at the speed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This speed is significantly slower than what is required to transport water efficiently in tall trees, where higher rates are necessary to reach the uppermost leaves.

Although this speed is somewhat closer to reality, it still falls short in accounting for the combined effects of root pressure, capillary action, and the powerful transpiration pull that facilitates faster water movement.

This speed accurately reflects the combined efficiency of root pressure, capillary action, and transpiration pull, which enables water to be transported at approximately 8 meters per hour in tall trees.
This speed overestimates the typical rate of water movement. While the mechanisms involved are efficient, they generally result in water transport at around 8 meters per hour, not 10.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 8mh-1. This speed captures the combined efficiency of root pressure, capillary action, and transpiration pull, which are the primary mechanisms that enable water to travel from the roots to the leaves in tall trees. Option A (2mh-1) and Option B (3mh-1) are too slow to support the needs of tall trees, as they underestimate the speed provided by these mechanisms. Option D (10mh-1) overestimates the speed, as the typical rate is closer to 8 meters per hour, reflecting the balance of forces involved in water transport.

questions.text: During the exposure to blue light, all of the following events occur, EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Under blue light, proton pumps are activated, leading to acidification of the environment. This process is an essential part of stomatal opening.
Blue light triggers the accumulation of potassium ions in guard cells, increasing their turgidity. This is crucial for the opening of stomata.

options.text: Uptake of K ions of guard cells
options.explanation: In response to blue light, guard cells actively transport potassium ions into the cells, contributing to increased turgor pressure and stomatal opening.
Blue light typically causes guard cells to become turgid, not flaccid. Flaccidity would result in stomatal closure, which is contrary to the usual response to blue light exposure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is “Flaccidity of guard cells.” Blue light stimulates the activation of proton pumps in guard cells, resulting in the acidification of their environment. This triggers the uptake of potassium ions, followed by water, increasing the turgor pressure within the cells and leading to stomatal opening. Thus, turgidity is the typical response to blue light, not flaccidity. The other options describe processes that align with the usual response of guard cells to blue light.

questions.text: Which organ of a plant has functions of anchoring, storage of food, absorbing water, and minerals:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The shoot of a plant typically refers to the above-ground portion, including stems, leaves, and reproductive structures such as flowers. While the shoot plays crucial roles in photosynthesis, transportation of water and nutrients, and reproduction, it is not primarily responsible for anchoring the plant or absorbing water and minerals from the soil.

Leaves are primarily responsible for photosynthesis, where they convert sunlight into chemical energy (food) for the plant. They also play a role in gas exchange. While leaves may absorb a minimal amount of water and minerals, they are not specialized for anchoring the plant or serving as the primary site for water and mineral absorption.

Flowers are reproductive structures of a plant and play a vital role in pollination and seed production. They attract pollinators for fertilization. Flowers are not involved in anchoring the plant, storing food, or absorbing water and minerals.

The root is the correct answer because it is the primary organ of a plant that performs the mentioned functions of anchoring, storage of food, and absorbing water, and minerals. Roots anchor the plant firmly in the soil, preventing it from being easily uprooted by wind or other external forces. They store excess carbohydrates, such as sugars and starches, produced during photosynthesis in the leaves. Roots are responsible for absorbing water and minerals from the soil.

questions.explanation: The root anchors the plant in the soil, absorbs water and minerals, and also stores food in some plants like carrots and radishes.

questions.text: The pressure exerted by the Swelling protoplast on the walls of the cell is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pressure placed on the contents of a plant cell by the cell wall that is equivalent to and opposite to the force of the turgor pressure is referred to as wall pressure.

Suction pressure is the negative difference in pressure between two places that drags liquid or gas from a higher pressure state to a lower pressure state. The suctional force in plants is caused by transpiration. This force aids the plant in obtaining essential minerals from the soil.

Osmotic pressure can be defined as the minimum pressure that must be applied to a solution to halt the flow of solvent molecules through a semipermeable membrane (osmosis). It is a colligative property and is dependent on the concentration of solute particles in the solution.

The pressure exerted by the swelling protoplast on the walls of the cell is called turgor pressure. Turgor pressure is what keeps plant cells from collapsing. It is also what gives plants their rigid structure.

questions.explanation: The pressure exerted by the swelling protoplast on the walls of the cell is called turgor pressure. Turgor pressure is what keeps plant cells from collapsing. It is also what gives plants their rigid structure.

questions.text: Companion cells are important in phloem tissue because they supply _ to sieve elements:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect. Water is mainly transported through the xylem, not the phloem. Companion cells do not primarily supply water to sieve elements.
Correct. Companion cells supply ATP (adenosine triphosphate) to sieve elements, providing the energy necessary for the active transport of nutrients like sugars within the phloem.
Incorrect. While carbohydrates are transported in the phloem, they are not directly supplied by companion cells to sieve elements. Instead, they are the main substances being transported.
Incorrect. Although proteins are present in the phloem, companion cells do not primarily supply proteins to sieve elements.

questions.explanation: Companion cells are crucial in phloem tissue because they provide ATP to sieve elements. ATP is essential for the active transport of sugars and other nutrients through the sieve tubes. While carbohydrates are the main substances transported within the phloem, they are not supplied directly by companion cells. Water and proteins are not the primary substances supplied to sieve elements by companion cells, which is why the correct answer is ATPs.

questions.text: The shrinkage of protoplast due to exosmosis is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The ascent of sap involves the upward movement of water and minerals through the xylem from roots to leaves. It does not involve shrinkage due to exosmosis.
Guttation is the exudation of water droplets from leaf tips, not related to the shrinkage of protoplasts or exosmosis.
Plasmolysis is when the protoplast shrinks away from the cell wall due to water loss through osmosis. This occurs when the external solution is hypertonic.
Deplasmolysis is the process of a plasmolyzed cell regaining water and turgor pressure, the opposite of shrinkage due to exosmosis.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Plasmolysis. Plasmolysis occurs when a plant cell loses water due to osmosis, causing the protoplast to shrink away from the cell wall in a hypertonic solution. This is in contrast to the Ascent of sap, which involves water movement in the xylem; Guttation, which involves water exudation from leaves; and Deplasmolysis, which is the rehydration of a plasmolyzed cell.

questions.text: A rye plant less than _ tall has branch roots about:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Rye plants are usually smaller than two meters, and their branch roots do not extend to such lengths when they are less than one meter tall.
This is incorrect. A rye plant does not typically reach five meters in height, nor do its branch roots extend this far.

This is the correct option. When a rye plant is less than one meter tall, it generally develops branch roots that can extend to about one meter, allowing the plant to efficiently absorb water and nutrients from the soil.
Even if a rye plant is less than half a meter tall, its branch roots would still extend to about one meter in length, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is “One meter”. A rye plant less than one meter tall can develop branch roots with a combined length of over 600 kilometers, allowing it to access a large volume of soil for nutrients and water. The other options describe unrealistic sizes for rye plants and their root systems when they are less than one meter tall.

questions.text: The casparian strips are present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. While the cortex is part of the root, the Casparian strips are not found here. They are located in the endodermis, which is a specialized layer within the cortex.

This is not correct. The pericycle is involved in the formation of lateral roots and does not contain Casparian strips, which are important for regulating water and solute entry into the vascular system.

This is correct. Casparian strips are found in the endodermis of roots. The strips form a hydrophobic barrier that regulates the movement of substances into the plant's vascular system, ensuring that all water and solutes must pass through the plasma membrane of the endodermal cells.
This is incorrect. The xylem is part of the vascular system responsible for the transport of water and nutrients, not for regulating their entry. Casparian strips are not present in the xylem.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is “Endodermis cells of roots”. Casparian strips are located in the endodermis, which serves as a selective barrier between the outer root tissues and the vascular tissue. This barrier ensures that all water and nutrients must pass through the endodermal cells, allowing the plant to control which substances enter its vascular system. The other options are incorrect: the cortex does not contain Casparian strips; the pericycle is involved in the formation of lateral roots; and the xylem is the transport tissue, not a barrier tissue.

questions.text: The force of attraction between water molecules is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adhesion refers to the force that attracts molecules of different substances to each other, such as water to plant cell walls. This is not the force responsible for water molecules attracting other water molecules.
Cohesion is the correct answer as it describes the force of attraction between water molecules. This force is due to hydrogen bonding, which helps maintain the integrity of water columns in plants.
Tensile strength relates to a material's ability to resist being pulled apart or stretched, not to the attraction between water molecules.
Imbibition involves a solid absorbing liquid, driven by both adhesion and cohesion, but it is not a term describing the attraction between water molecules themselves.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is “Cohesion.” Cohesion is the force of attraction between like molecules, such as water molecules. This force arises from hydrogen bonds, maintaining water's structure as it moves through plant tissues. Adhesion, on the other hand, involves attraction between different materials, and tensile strength pertains to resistance to pulling forces. Imbibition, while involving cohesion, more specifically refers to the absorption process, not the attraction between water molecules.

questions.text: Which one of the following is involved in the closing of stomata?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gibberellins are primarily involved in promoting growth processes such as stem elongation and seed germination, not in stomatal closure.
Ethylene is a plant hormone associated with fruit ripening and response to stress, but it does not play a major role in stomatal closure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Abscisic acid. In a hypertonic solution, the solute potential is lower and the water potential is lower than that of the cell. Water moves out of the cell, causing it to lose water and shrink.

questions.text: The force exerted by protoplast against the cell wall is called _ potential.

options.explanation: Osmotic potential is related to the movement of water across membranes due to solute concentration differences. It does not directly involve the force exerted by the protoplast against the cell wall.

Pressure potential refers to the mechanical force that the protoplast (living part of the cell) exerts against the cell wall. This pressure is crucial for maintaining the structure and rigidity of plant cells.
A generator is a device that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. It is not related to the forces within plant cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is “Pressure”. Pressure potential is the force exerted by the protoplast against the cell wall, contributing to the turgor pressure that helps maintain plant cell structure. A generator is unrelated to this biological context.

questions.text: Which one of the following has an open circulatory system?

options.explanation: Octopuses have a closed circulatory system with a chambered heart. However, unlike humans, their blood does not flow through arteries and veins. Hemocyanin is similar to hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying pigment in human blood, but it contains copper instead of iron.
Hirudinaria, or leeches, have a closed circulatory system.

Periplaneta, commonly known as the American cockroach, does have an open circulatory system. Open circulatory systems are found in insects and other invertebrates. In an open circulatory system, the blood flows freely in the body cavity and bathes the organs directly. There is no heart to pump the blood through the body. In Periplaneta, the blood flows through a series of tubes called hemocoel. The hemocoel is divided into two chambers: the pericardial sinus and the perivisceral sinus. The pericardial sinus surrounds the heart, and the perivisceral sinus surrounds the organs.

questions.text: The semilunar valves are located at the base of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The semilunar valves, specifically the pulmonary valve and the aortic valve, are positioned at the exit points of the ventricles where the major arteries begin. The pulmonary semilunar valve is at the base of the pulmonary artery, and the aortic semilunar valve is at the base of the aorta, preventing backflow of blood into the ventricles.

The vena cava (superior and inferior) are large veins that drain deoxygenated blood into the right atrium, and they do not have a semilunar valve at their base. Blood flow from the vena cava into the atrium is generally unimpeded, unlike the high-pressure exit from the ventricles.

Pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs into the left atrium, and they also lack a true valve structure at their entrance to the heart. The left atrial contraction itself helps minimize backflow into the veins.

Coronary arteries branch off the ascending aorta immediately above the aortic semilunar valve, but they are relatively small vessels and do not have a semilunar valve at their base. Their openings are located within the larger structure controlled by the aortic valve.

questions.explanation: These valves prevent backflow of blood into the ventricles during diastole.

questions.text: At start of ventricular contraction:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Bi and tricuspid valves are opened
options.explanation: No, the bicuspid and tricuspid valves are not opened at the start of ventricular contraction. The bicuspid and tricuspid valves are located between the atria and ventricles of the heart. They open during atrial systole, which is the contraction of the atria, to allow blood to flow from the atria to the ventricles. At the start of ventricular contraction, the ventricles are still relaxed, and the pressure in the ventricles is lower than the pressure in the atria. This keeps the bicuspid and tricuspid valves closed, preventing blood from flowing back into the atria.

The semilunar valves are closed at the start of ventricular contraction. This is because the pressure in the ventricles is still lower than the pressure in the arteries. The semilunar valves only open when the pressure in the ventricles is higher than the pressure in the arteries. The semilunar valves are located between the ventricles and the arteries. They are responsible for preventing blood from flowing back into the ventricles from the arteries.

options.text: Bi and tricuspid valves are opened and semilunar valves are closed
options.explanation: No, at the start of ventricular contraction, the bi and tricuspid valves are closed and the semilunar valves are open. The bi and tricuspid valves are located between the atria and ventricles. They allow blood to flow from the atria to the ventricles during diastole. At the start of ventricular contraction, the pressure in the ventricles increases, which closes the bi and tricuspid valves. This prevents blood from flowing back into the atria. The semilunar valves are located between the ventricles and the arteries. They allow blood to flow from the ventricles to the arteries during systole. At the start of ventricular contraction, the pressure in the ventricles is still lower than the pressure in the arteries. This keeps the semilunar valves closed. As the ventricles contract and the pressure inside them increases, the semilunar valves open and allow blood to flow out of the heart. The closing of the bi and tricuspid valves and the opening of the semilunar valves create the first heart sound. This sound is heard as a "lub-dub" sound. The "lub" sound is caused by the closing of the bi and tricuspid valves, and the "dub" sound is caused by the opening of the semilunar valves.

options.text: Bi and tricuspid valves are closed and semilunar valves are opened
options.explanation: At the start of ventricular contraction, the biventricular heart valves (mitral and tricuspid valves) are closed and the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic valves) are opened. This allows the ventricles to contract and pump blood out of the heart. The mitral valve connects the left atrium to the left ventricle, and the tricuspid valve connects the right atrium to the right ventricle. When these valves are closed, they prevent blood from flowing back into the atria during ventricular contraction.
